title: Why Everyone Online Explains Complex Math Concepts Better Than My Professor
author: Tklesson Team
content:
You are sitting in a calculus lecture. The professor is at the board, writing equations. You are copying them down, but you are lost. The explanation is moving too fast, or it assumes you remember something from a prerequisite you took two years ago, or it is using notation you have never seen before. You leave the lecture feeling like you are the only one who did not understand. Then you go home, search the topic on YouTube, and find a ten-minute video that explains the exact same concept with clarity and patience. Suddenly it clicks. Why did that take ten minutes online and a full lecture in person? And why does the online explanation feel so much more effective?

The Structural Difference Between a Lecture and a Video

A university lecture and an educational video are fundamentally different formats with different goals. The professor is teaching a course. They have a semester's worth of material to cover, a department-mandated syllabus to follow, and a room full of students with widely varying levels of preparation. They are explaining the concept once, in real time, with no ability to edit or refine their explanation after the fact. If they go too fast, they cannot re-record. If they assume too much background knowledge, they cannot add a clarifying section later.

The video creator is teaching a single concept. They chose that concept because they know students struggle with it. They wrote a script. They edited it. They recorded it, probably multiple times. They added visuals that illustrate exactly what they are describing. They paced the explanation for an audience that can pause, rewind, and rewatch. They are not responsible for covering an entire curriculum or preparing you for an exam. They are responsible for making one thing make sense. That is a much easier job, and they are optimized for it.

Why Videos Feel More Effective

Educational videos use specific techniques that improve comprehension. Visuals are synchronized with audio, which activates both visual and auditory processing channels. Animations show processes unfolding over time rather than asking you to imagine them from static diagrams. Examples are concrete and worked through step by step. The pace is controlled. You can pause to think. You can rewatch the part you did not understand the first time.

Your professor knows the material so deeply that they have forgotten what it feels like to not understand it. This is called the expert blind spot. They cannot easily put themselves in the mind of a student encountering the concept for the first time. The video creator often learned the concept recently themselves, or they teach it so frequently that they have seen every common point of confusion. Their explanations are built around those points of confusion because they know exactly where students get stuck.

This Does Not Mean Your Professor Is Bad

It is easy to conclude that the video creator is simply a better teacher than your professor. That conclusion is usually wrong. Teaching a single concept on video is not the same job as teaching a semester-long course. Your professor has to sequence topics so they build on each other. They have to design assessments that fairly measure understanding. They have to answer questions in real time during office hours. They have to manage a classroom, accommodate different learning needs, and evaluate your work. The video creator does none of these things.

Your professor also does not have the luxury of only teaching the topics they are best at explaining. Every course contains some topics that are harder to teach than others. The video creator chose to make a video about the topic they can explain well. They skipped the topics they cannot. That selection bias makes them look like a better teacher than they might be across an entire course.

How to Use Both Resources Effectively

The smartest students do not choose between the professor and the online resource. They use both strategically. Attend the lecture. Take notes. Identify the specific points where you got lost. Then find a video that addresses those points. The video fills in the gaps in your understanding. The lecture provides the structure, the syllabus alignment, and the indication of what will be on the exam. Together, they are more powerful than either alone.

Go to office hours with your remaining questions. Tell your professor you watched a video that explained the concept differently and ask if your understanding is now correct. Professors respect students who take initiative to learn from multiple sources. They may even recommend specific online resources that align with their teaching approach.

The Bottom Line

The educational video creator and your professor are not in competition. They are doing different jobs. The video creator teaches one concept at a time, with editing and visuals, optimized for a general audience. Your professor teaches an entire course, in real time, optimized for the specific curriculum and exam. Use both. Attend lectures for structure and alignment with course expectations. Use online videos to fill in the gaps. The combination is a learning system more powerful than either resource alone.
